title: The creators start a company
content: In 2013, the team behind Spark founded Databricks. Their goal was to reduce Spark's biggest pain point: infrastructure management. The idea was a managed cloud service where users focus on data work while the platform handles most of the machine setup and operations.
next: What was the first thing they made easy?

title: Clusters with a click
content: Setting up a Spark cluster used to take specialist knowledge and time. On Databricks, you create one with a few clicks. The platform provisions the machines, configures Spark, and manages much of the operational setup so you can start working faster.
next: What stops those machines from wasting money?

title: It scales itself
content: Databricks clusters can autoscale: they add machines when workload rises and remove them when demand drops. They can also terminate when idle, which reduces wasted spend. This does not eliminate cost decisions, but it makes resource control easier.
next: Where do people actually write their code?

title: Built around notebooks
content: The heart of the workspace is the notebook: a document where you write code in cells and see results and charts right below them. You can mix Python, SQL, Scala, and R, add notes, and edit together with teammates in real time.
next: Is it just plain Spark running underneath?

title: A better Spark, the Databricks Runtime
content: Underneath runs the Databricks Runtime: an optimized version of Spark with performance, reliability, and security improvements built in. It also includes components such as Photon for faster query execution in some workloads. This gives teams a stronger default setup with less manual tuning.
next: Could they make compute even simpler than 'one click'?

title: Serverless: forget the cluster
content: Serverless compute goes one step further: users think less about cluster setup and more about the actual workload. Databricks supplies the compute, scales it to demand, and charges based on usage. The infrastructure does not disappear completely, but much more of it moves into the background.
next: How do you run this work automatically, every day?

title: Jobs put it on autopilot
content: A job schedules and orchestrates work. It runs tasks — notebooks, queries, or pipelines — in the right order, on a schedule or when triggered, with automatic retries and alerts if something fails. This is how data work becomes reliable production.
next: Can you manage all of this like real software?

title: Your data project, as code
content: With asset bundles, you describe jobs and pipelines in YAML configuration files. That means data projects can be version-controlled, reviewed, and deployed more reliably. It brings software engineering practices such as CI/CD closer to data work.
next: Where does all of this actually run?

title: Native to the cloud
content: Databricks runs on the major clouds. On Microsoft's cloud it's a first-party service called Azure Databricks, integrated directly with Azure storage, security, and identity. You get the platform without leaving your cloud environment.
next: Who gets to use all this in one place?

title: Everyone in one workspace
content: Data engineers, analysts, and data scientists can work in the same platform on the same underlying data. Instead of stitching together many separate tools, teams collaborate in one environment. This is one of the main reasons unified platforms appeal to organizations.
next: So is the data problem finally solved?

title: The engine was easy — the data wasn't
content: Databricks made Spark easy to run. But the deeper problem remained: data still lived in messy, unreliable lakes or rigid, closed warehouses. To truly fix things, Databricks would have to rethink where the data itself should live.
next: Their answer combined the best of both worlds: the lakehouse.
